data.title: Biogen downgraded to hold by Jefferies, Ocrevus royalties cited
data.description:
Biogen (NASDAQ:BIIB) was downgraded to hold from buy by Jefferies, due in part to a looming royalty cut for the multiple sclerosis drug Ocrevus.

Jefferies said it downgraded the stock on three issues: anticipated modest sales of Leqembi, lack of a pipeline that enthuses investors, and an expected 50% step down in royalties for Ocrevus in 2030.

The investment bank added that Biogen (BIIB) needs more pipeline candidates and business development to help offset the 2030 step down for Ocrevus.

Ocrevus is marketed by Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) Genentech subsidiary.

Jefferies lowered its price target for Biogen (BIIB) to $180 from $250.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery enters content sharing agreement with Comcast
data.description:
Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) has entered a multi-year deal with Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA) to provide its portfolio of content to Xfinity and Sky UK and Ireland subscribers, along with continued carriage of HBO, and expand Comcast’s right to package the ad-supported versions of Max and Discovery+ within its streaming bundles. Comcast (CMCSA) will also continue to offer Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) content as part of its U.S. NOW TV streaming service.

“Through these agreements, we will bring Warner Bros. Discovery’s extensive portfolio to our customers however they want to consume the content across our existing and future linear television and streaming bundles,” said Comcast’s president of contact acquisition.

The Warner Bros. Discovery/Comcast agreement will also include a new, non-exclusive ad-supported Max app when WBD launches the service in the UK and Ireland in early 2026.

According to Deadline, the deal appears to settle what was a “contentious” situation in the UK, where Max is set to roll out into Sky territory in 2026 amid an ongoing international expansion, and after its HBO output deal with Sky expires. Last September, Comcast (CMCSA) sued Warner Bros. (WBD) for failing to comply with the terms of its five-year output deal between HBO and Sky in the UK, Italy and Germany. By agreeing to the above terms, the lawsuit against WBD is now moot.

Financial terms of the deal were not disclosed.

Shares of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) were propped up to an 11-month-high.

data.title: Man questioned in Pennsylvania in killing of UnitedHealthcare CEO: reports (update)
Onity Group upped to Buy at BTIG on better earnings visibility
data.description:
BTIG upgraded Onity Group (NYSE:ONIT) stock to Buy from Neutral on Monday on the view its more stable leverage will improve earnings visibility.

The mortgage loan company, formerly known as Ocwen, has been pushing to stabilize its capital structure and lean towards a capital-light model, analyst Eric Hagen wrote in a note.

"The opportunity is buying the stock as its earnings visibility improves because of the more stable leverage, which marries nicely with its operational strength as a high-touch servicer for the growing community of non-bank MSR investors, including REITs and other credit funds," the note said.

"At its current valuation we don't think the company needs to necessarily make a lot of money to see some valuation improvement, but rather show some consistency in not losing money, especially when peers put up good results."

BTIG's Buy rating aligns with the SA Quant system rating of Strong Buy and the average Wall Street analyst rating of Buy.

(NYSE:ONIT) shares gained 0.6% in afternoon trading.

data.title: 30% of U.S. consumers will increase holiday buys due to future tariff fears – survey
data.description:
About a third of Americans are increasing purchases this holiday season – November and December – due to fear of future tariffs on imports, according to a December consumer survey report by CreditCards.com.

The survey, which interviewed 2,000 U.S. residents, showed that 39% of respondents fear future higher prices from the potential increase in tariffs by the Trump administration.

About 25% of respondents who said they will increase their spending this season said they expect supply chain disruptions, and about 20% said they have concerns about societal instability. Another 20% said they fear a recession.

Of those respondents who plan to make large purchases, 67% said they will buy electronics, 37% said home appliances, 32% said furniture, 24% said home improvement materials, and 22% said cars.

In addition, 34% of respondents said they are “doom spending” and about 30% are “likely to go into or worsen debt to secure purchases now.”

“Some consumers, particularly those influenced by presidential politics and economic news, have remained pessimistic about the economy despite data indicating the post-pandemic economy has improved,” said John Egan, credit cards, insurance, and personal finance expert contributor at CreditCards.com. “Nonetheless, overall consumer spending has gone up recently, thanks in part to rising wages and low unemployment.”

Lastly, 34% of respondents said they are stockpiling items due to fear and uncertainty about the future. Of those, 77% said they are stockpiling toilet paper, 76% said they are stockpiling non-perishable foods, and 58% said medical supplies, including gloves and hand sanitizer.

The following are the top consumer stocks – rated by Seeking Alpha quant metrics – including consumer electronics, home furnishing, home improvement, and automotive retail.

data.title: Mobileye rises after RBC Capital points to company being close to winning a contract
data.description:
Update: The story was updated with comments from General Motors.

Mobileye Global (NASDAQ:MBLY) stock rose about 6% on Monday after RBC Capital said that the company could be close to winning a contract, following the release of a presentation as part of the company's investor event in Munich.

Analyst Tom Narayan said that one of the slides in the presentation could suggest the company is close to a nomination for a Japanese original equipment manufacturer, or OEM. Another slide indicates that the next to nomination stage says "2-3 months." Also included is a European OEM, the company could be close to a nomination for Surround ADAS. A U.S. OEM is noted as within the negotiation and decision-making step. This comes before the nomination step, the analyst added.

Narayan noted that while not an award yet, the stage this Japanese OEM is at, could mean it is close to being won. He added that in their conversations with investors, this is the number one question — if Mobileye will announce an OEM SuperVision, or SV, win.

SuperVision is the company's advanced driver-assist system.

Narayan said they were not expecting a win announcement at the event but the possibility that the company could be close is a positive.

The analyst thinks that the four Japanese OEMs, which could be in the mix, are Honda (HMC), Toyota (TM), Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) (OTCPK:NSANF) and Mazda (OTCPK:MZDAY) (OTCPK:MZDAF). A Honda or Toyota win could be significant, according to the analyst, due to volumes.

The analyst wonders if the U.S. OEM in the negotiating phase for Surround ADAS could be General Motors (GM) and the European OEM close to nomination for Surround ADAS could be Stellantis (STLA) or Renault (OTCPK:RNSDF).

Mobileye did not respond to a query from Seeking Alpha till the time of publication of this report.

"We're not going to engage in the rumor and speculation," said General Motors in an emailed statement to Seeking Alpha.

Honda, Toyota, Nissan, Stellantis and Renault also did not respond to a query from Seeking Alpha till the time this report was published.

data.title: Carrier is among industrials at risk from Trump tariffs, Deutsche Bank says
data.description:
Carrier Global (NYSE:CARR) and Lennox International (NYSE:LII) are among the makers of industrial products or electrical equipment that face the greatest risk from President-elect Donald Trump’s possible tariffs on imports, according to analysts at financial-services firm Deutsche Bank.

Zurn Elkay Water Solutions (NYSE:ZWS), Stanley Black & Decker (NYSE:SWK) and Badger Meter (NYSE:BMI) also are at risk, Nicole DeBlase, analyst at Deutsche Bank, said in a December 8 report.

“We combed through multi-industry and electrical equipment companies' filings and recent transcripts in an attempt to gain insight into how new tariffs on China, Mexico and/or Canada might impact the group,” she said. “The high-level takeaway was that most companies manufacture in region for-region, so the tariff impact should generally be modest/manageable, albeit with a few exceptions.”

Imposing tariffs on imports was a key pledge of the Trump campaign as part of an effort to help rebuild the U.S. manufacturing base. It’s not clear whether the threat of tariffs is merely a negotiating ploy, or that Trump intends to follow through on his plan.

Trump on November 25 posted on his Truth Social account that he would enact a 25% tariff on goods coming into the U.S. from Mexico and Canada. The next day, he said he might impose an additional 10% tariff on products imported from China.”

Multi-Industry & Electrical Equipment Companies at Risk for Tariffs - Deutsche Bank, Dec. 8
Company (Ticker) Key Risks
Carrier Global (CARR) "The company has a significant manufacturing site in Monterrey which would likely be subject to tariffs on exports to the U.S., though it is unclear what the exact size of the potential headwind may be."
Lennox International (LII) "Mexico accounts for 19% of the total company's manufacturing facility square footage and ~32% of its PP&E."
Zurn Elkay (ZWS) "Looking at China sourcing exposure, there is a more meaningful imbalance that would be subject to tariffs as basically all of it is sold into North America."
Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) "China makes up a large portion of the U.S. cost of goods sold base at 20%-25%, with 45%-50% from the U.S. and 25-30% from rest of world."
Badger Meter (BMI) "The company discloses that 4% of total assets are in Mexico and of its manufacturing square footage, 28% is located in its Nogales, Mexico, facility ... it is fair to assume there is a sizable portion of production that is exported into the U.S., which likely puts the company at some risk related to Mexico tariffs."

Source: Deutsche Bank

data.title: Jefferies, Moelis raised, Lazard, HLI cut at Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Morgan Stanley upgraded Moelis (NYSE:MC) and Jefferies Financial (NYSE:JEF) to Overweight from Equalweight have bolstered ranks to prepare for an M&A upturn. Meanwhile, the firm downgraded Lazard (NYSE:LAZ) to Equalweight, citing valuation, and Houlihan Lokey (NYSE:HLI) to Underweight as it has less to gain from an increase in a large-cap M&A rebound.

The firm expects more clarity from the incoming Trump administration on antitrust enforcement policy in early 2025 toward "a more traditional, transparent approach." That should inspire CEOs to engage in deals as animal spirits rise. This comes as the Federal Reserve is cutting rates because inflation is subsiding, not in response to a weak economy. "We view this as a major positive for M&A activity, as lower debt financing costs in a soft landing environment should incentivize sponsors to deploy dry powder and corporates to deploy excess capital," Morgan Stanley analyst Ryan Kenny wrote in a note to clients. "Together, these events push our M&A outlook closer to our prior bull case."

Jefferies (NYSE:JEF) is poised for significant share gains as its managing director count rose 46% from Q4 2021 and 72% from Q4 2019. "In addition to Advisory, JEF is focused on taking further share in its Equities and Fixed Income trading businesses," Kenny said. "JEF plans to expand their Equities TAM over time from $15-20B to $50-60B by enhancing JEF’s prime brokerage offering, growing swap balances, offeringmore structured derivatives products and expanding program trading."

Moelis's (NYSE:MC) compensation ratio increased the most among Morgan Stanley's coverage over the last several years, "and has the most room to inflect, driving significant earnings revisions," the analyst wrote. "We estimate that this leverage, fueled by revenue improvement, will drive a 380% improvement in EPS in 2026 vs. 2024."

Lazard's (NYSE:LAZ) downgrade partly reflects valuation, as its stock price more than doubled from its trough in late 2023. Kenny now sees the shares' risk-reward as more balanced. "In the asset management business, which skews to emerging market equities, we see risk of continued outflows given recent outflows have been larger than expected for several quarters in a row, recent US equity market outperformance and risks of a stronger dollar under a Trump administration," the analyst said.

Houlihan Lokey (NYSE:HLI), meanwhile, as a middle market investment bank, doesn't traditionally advise on mega cap or large cap deals. "As a result, we see less of a benefit from a potentially more favorable antitrust environment." And Morgan Stanley models flattish revenue from HLI's restructuring business, expecting that the company's peak growth rate in restructuring has passed.

None of the four stocks mentioned get a Buy rating from the SA Quant system. Meanwhile, Jefferies (JEF) and Lazard (LAZ) get Buy ratings from the average Wall Street analyst, Houlihan Lokey (HLI) is rated Hold, and the average Wall Street rating for Moelis (MC) is Sell.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: C.H. Robinson Worldwide is expected to ride AI initiatives to improved productivity
data.description:
C.H. Robinson Worldwide (NASDAQ:CHRW) motored higher on Monday after Wells Fargo upgraded the stock to an Overweight rating after having it slotted at Equal-weight.

Analyst Chris Wetherbee and his team see a unique opportunity for earnings to compound through 2027, driven by improved execution through tech initiatives. At the company's upcoming investor day, CHRW management is expected to outline that productivity can improve through the implementation of new technology and AI.

"Automation should drive more productivity gains and support a return to ~40% NAST operating margins through the cycle. We expect this to be a key pillar of guidance, and it's a driver of our ~$7.50 in '27 EPS bull case scenario."

Over the long term, the opportunity is seen being even bigger, as AI potentially allows for step function gains in productivity of 30% to 50%. Notably, Wells Fargo's bull case price target on CHRW of ~$7.50 is almost 30% higher than the Street.

Shares of C.H. Robinson Worldwide (CHRW) were up 2.28% at 1:33 p.m. to trade within 5% of the 52-week share price high. Wall Street and Seeking Alpha analysts have a consensus Hold rating on CHRW.

data.title: T-Mobile CEO warns investors of risks in second half of Q4
data.description:
Shares of T-Mobile (NASDAQ:TMUS) turned defensive Monday after the company’s CEO warned of risks to the business in the second half of the current quarter.

“It's been a great quarter so far. The only thing I would caution, and I can just hear myself saying this in prior years as well, is that this quarter's back end loaded. So, while I'm here to tell you it's going great, we're confident in our guidance, investors should be cautious because there's a lot of risk in the back half,” said CEO Michael Sievert at a UBS conference, adding that device upgrade rate has been “pretty low.”

Late October, the company said it expected postpaid net customer additions to be between 5.6M and 5.8M, revised from the prior guidance of 5.4M to 5.7M and adjusted EBITDA between $31.6B and $31.8B versus a consensus estimate of $31.6B.

T-Mobile (TMUS) shares are down more than 5%, challenging support at the 50-day moving average.
